name: Nala
description: Nala is a virtuous king of the Nishadha Kingdom, renowned for his dharma, skill with horses, and culinary expertise. He is best known for his epic romance and trials alongside Damayanti, a princess of Vidarbha. Nala's story is primarily narrated in the Vana Parva of the Mahabharata, where his tale serves as a cautionary yet ultimately uplifting narrative for Yudhishthira during his own period of exile and hardship. Nala's adherence to truthfulness and dharma is tested severely by the influence of Kali, the personification of the Kali Yuga, who seeks to corrupt him. His temporary downfall, marked by gambling and separation from Damayanti, illustrates the power of negative influences, while his eventual restoration highlights the enduring strength of virtue and the possibility of redemption. He is also a skilled charioteer and is sometimes associated with water. Nala's devotion to Damayanti and his eventual reunion with her after enduring separation and hardship is a testament to the power of love and fidelity. His story remains a powerful example of resilience, righteousness, and the triumph of good over evil within the Hindu tradition.
subtitle: The Virtuous King

keyMyths.title: Nala and Damayanti's Love Story
keyMyths.description: Nala and Damayanti's love story begins with Hamsa, a golden swan, acting as a messenger between them, carrying their mutual admiration. Damayanti, facing a Swayamvara (self-choice ceremony), chooses Nala over the gods Indra, Agni, Varuna, and Yama, who are impressed by his virtue and step aside. This choice highlights Damayanti's discernment and Nala's exceptional qualities, setting the stage for their subsequent trials. The myth emphasizes the power of love and the importance of choosing a partner based on virtue rather than power or divine status. Their union is initially blessed, but soon faces the test of Kali's influence.

keyMyths.title: Nala's Downfall and Exile
keyMyths.description: Under the influence of Kali, Nala loses his kingdom to his brother Pushkara in a dice game. He is forced into exile with Damayanti, enduring immense hardship. During their exile, Nala abandons Damayanti in a forest, driven by Kali's influence and his own insecurities. He takes on the role of a charioteer for King Rituparna, demonstrating his skills with horses but concealing his true identity. This period of exile tests Nala's character and his adherence to dharma, showcasing the destructive power of negative influences and the challenges of maintaining virtue in adversity. The myth highlights the importance of self-control and the consequences of succumbing to temptation.

keyMyths.title: Nala's Redemption and Reunion
keyMyths.description: After years of separation and hardship, Damayanti orchestrates a second Swayamvara, knowing that Nala, despite his altered appearance and circumstances, would be drawn to it. Recognizing him, she reveals his true identity. Through this process and a second dice game with Pushkara, Nala overcomes Kali's influence and regains his kingdom. He is reunited with Damayanti, their love and fidelity reaffirmed. This myth underscores the themes of redemption, the enduring power of love, and the triumph of good over evil. Nala's eventual victory symbolizes the possibility of overcoming even the most severe trials through perseverance and adherence to dharma.

sources.author: Ganguli, Kisari Mohan
